### II.1 Multipolar AI pathway

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Cut hard. Two stories (racing / gradual disempowerment) that differ mainly on evidentiary frame. Critch is the number-hides-mechanism case (~35% loss-of-control + ~50% dehumanization). Toner/Russell/Ord demoted to a side note — Russell really belongs at Architectural Agency, Ord is a time-horizon figure. Links: Carlsmith, Gradual Disempowerment, Critch.
:::

Assuming powerful AI does raise existential risk, the multipolar pathway covers the threats that arise from many systems interacting. It is one of the larger nodes, a hodgepodge that collects many of the real-world risks and implementations of even weak AI. The clearest example is racing that destabilizes global security. Racing that produces a single strong winner which then causes catastrophe belongs in the single-dominant node instead. What lives here are the racing failures that never produce a single strong optimizer but still elevate global tensions, often by incorporating AI too hastily into military or economic decisions.

Another failure mode here is gradual disempowerment, where capable systems, not necessarily misaligned, accrete economic and political power until human control becomes a formality, with no single villain and no sharp takeover. Carlsmith makes this argument in [his work on power-seeking AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353), and the [Gradual Disempowerment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.16946) paper develops the same idea, tracing how humans could be sidelined incrementally as automated systems take over economic and political functions.

Andrew Critch sits here too. He has [estimated an 85% p(doom)](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Kobbt3nQgv3yn29pr/my-motivation-and-theory-of-change-for-working-in-ai) but expects us to "dodge" a fast single takeover, only to succumb to a slower gradual disempowerment that sees humans outcompeted and sidelined by an economy that no longer needs them. He [splits his p(doom) between loss of control and economic disempowerment](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Kobbt3nQgv3yn29pr/my-motivation-and-theory-of-change-for-working-in-ai).
